explicit_text: I was born in Yamaguchi-Ken, Japan, in 1881, and grew up there with seven siblings. My father was a farmer, and although he wasn’t strict, he worked very hard until he died young from pneumonia. Life in Yamaguchi was simple; I went to elementary school for four years and enjoyed playing by the sea and in the mountains. My family had a mix of occupations, including carpentry and farming, and some of my brothers moved away, including one who lived in Taiwan.
implicit_text: I was born in a rural area of western Japan in 1881, and grew up there with seven siblings. My father was a farmer, and although he wasn’t strict, he worked very hard until he died young from pneumonia. Life in that region was simple; I went to elementary school for four years and enjoyed playing by the sea and in the mountains. My family had a mix of occupations, including carpentry and farming, and some of my brothers moved away, including one who lived in a nearby island territory.

explicit_text: One of my brothers lived in Taiwan, which was called Formosa back then, and he worked at a sugar factory. Unfortunately, he died in an accident while working there. This was during the time when some Japanese were moving to places like Taiwan for work opportunities. It was a difficult loss for our family.
implicit_text: One of my brothers lived in an island territory in East Asia, which was called Formosa back then, and he worked at a sugar factory. Unfortunately, he died in an accident while working there. This was during the time when some Japanese were moving to places like that island for work opportunities. It was a difficult loss for our family.

explicit_text: I came to Hawaii around 1898 when I was about 17 years old, joining many Japanese who were moving there at the time. I worked in the sugar cane fields under a contract for about three years, but the drought made the crops poor. While waiting for transportation to a small island, I caught typhoid fever and was very sick, but a kind nurse helped me recover. I also enjoyed horse racing there and even owned a horse that I raised from a pony.
implicit_text: I came to a group of islands in the central Pacific around 1898 when I was about 17 years old, joining many Japanese who were moving there at the time. I worked in the sugar cane fields under a contract for about three years, but the drought made the crops poor. While waiting for transportation to a smaller island, I caught typhoid fever and was very sick, but a kind nurse helped me recover. I also enjoyed horse racing there and even owned a horse that I raised from a pony.

explicit_text: I left Japan during the Meiji period, specifically in Meiji 31, which is 1898. This was a time of great change in Japan, with modernization and increased overseas migration. I was about 17 years old when I decided to leave for Hawaii and later the mainland United States. The Meiji era shaped much of my early life and the opportunities I sought abroad.
implicit_text: I left Japan during a time of great change, specifically in the 31st year of the era that began in 1868, which is 1898. This was a time of modernization and increased overseas migration. I was about 17 years old when I decided to leave for the Pacific islands and later the mainland United States. That era shaped much of my early life and the opportunities I sought abroad.

explicit_text: I traveled on the steamship named Sonoma from Hawaii to San Francisco, which took about a week. It was an American coal-powered ship, and I helped move coal to the boiler room because it was better than doing nothing. The journey was uneventful, and the Sonoma was my passage to the mainland where I eventually settled.
implicit_text: I traveled on an American coal-powered steamship from the Pacific islands to the west coast of the United States, which took about a week. It was a coal-powered ship, and I helped move coal to the boiler room because it was better than doing nothing. The journey was uneventful, and this vessel was my passage to the mainland where I eventually settled.

explicit_text: When I first arrived in Hawaii and was waiting to go to a small island, I caught typhoid fever. I was very sick with high fever and dysentery, and many others were dying around me. Fortunately, a kind nurse cared for me by feeding me cooked rice liquid, and I recovered. That illness was a major hardship but also marked a turning point as my ear troubles healed afterward.
implicit_text: When I first arrived in the Pacific islands and was waiting to go to a small island, I caught a serious bacterial infection. I was very sick with high fever and dysentery, and many others were dying around me. Fortunately, a kind nurse cared for me by feeding me cooked rice liquid, and I recovered. That illness was a major hardship but also marked a turning point as my ear troubles healed afterward.

explicit_text: While in Hawaii, I remember racing my horse against a Portuguese man's horse. The Portuguese people were part of the diverse community there, and we sometimes went to horse races together and made bets. It was a time of some fun despite the hard work on the sugar plantations.
implicit_text: While in the Pacific islands, I remember racing my horse against a man from a European ethnic group that was part of the diverse community there. We sometimes went to horse races together and made bets. It was a time of some fun despite the hard work on the sugar plantations.

explicit_text: After leaving Hawaii, I arrived in San Francisco aboard the Sonoma steamship. From there, I traveled to Watsonville to join relatives and begin work on a strawberry farm. San Francisco was my gateway to the mainland United States and the start of a new chapter in my life.
implicit_text: After leaving the Pacific islands, I arrived in a major west coast city aboard the steamship. From there, I traveled to a smaller agricultural town to join relatives and begin work on a strawberry farm. That city was my gateway to the mainland United States and the start of a new chapter in my life.

explicit_text: I settled in Watsonville because I had relatives there, and I worked on my sister-in-law’s brother’s strawberry farm. Later, I operated my own farm growing strawberries on 20 to 21 acres. Watsonville was home for me and my family for many years, where I raised my children and rebuilt after setbacks like the Great Depression and World War II.
implicit_text: I settled in a small city in California because I had relatives there, and I worked on my sister-in-law’s brother’s strawberry farm. Later, I operated my own farm growing strawberries on 20 to 21 acres. That town was home for me and my family for many years, where I raised my children and rebuilt after setbacks like the Great Depression and World War II.

explicit_text: My sister-in-law’s brother was in the reserve of the Japanese Army and had to return to Japan to fight in the Russo-Japanese War. I remember sending him off at the Palo Alto station, waving a Japanese flag. Japan did call on reserves who were living abroad during that time, which affected our family directly.
implicit_text: My sister-in-law’s brother was in the reserve of the military forces of my home country and had to return there to fight in a war against Russia. I remember sending him off at a train station in California, waving a flag of our homeland. The military did call on reserves who were living abroad during that time, which affected our family directly.

explicit_text: The Russo-Japanese War broke out when I was already in America, but it still touched my family. My sister-in-law’s brother was called back to Japan to fight, and my older brother came to operate the farm in his place. It was a difficult time, but we managed to keep the farm going.
implicit_text: The war between Russia and my home country broke out when I was already in America, but it still touched my family. My sister-in-law’s brother was called back to fight, and my older brother came to operate the farm in his place. It was a difficult time, but we managed to keep the farm going.

explicit_text: I remember standing at the Palo Alto station to see off my sister-in-law’s brother who was returning to Japan to serve in the army during the Russo-Japanese War. I held a Japanese flag and waved it as the train left. It was a poignant moment reflecting the ties we still had to Japan despite living in America.
implicit_text: I remember standing at a train station in California to see off my sister-in-law’s brother who was returning to serve in the military during the war with Russia. I held a flag of our homeland and waved it as the train left. It was a poignant moment reflecting the ties we still had to our country despite living in America.

explicit_text: For about half a year, I worked near Monterey in a place called Sur, where we dug out lime. I cooked for about 30 Japanese men working there, and we transported lime from the hillside to boats. I also remember the sound of dynamite blasts from road construction nearby. It was a different kind of work compared to farming.
implicit_text: For about half a year, I worked near a coastal city in California in a place called Sur, where we dug out lime. I cooked for about 30 Japanese men working there, and we transported lime from the hillside to boats. I also remember the sound of dynamite blasts from road construction nearby. It was a different kind of work compared to farming.

explicit_text: During the Great Depression, especially around 1933, I managed to do alright despite the hard times. Earlier, in 1915, I had bought 50 acres and tried to expand strawberry farming, hoping to benefit from the World's Fair, but that venture lost money. I had to break up the partnership and start small again, gradually recovering my losses. The Depression was tough but I was able to keep farming.
implicit_text: During the economic crisis of the 1930s, especially around 1933, I managed to do alright despite the hard times. Earlier, in 1915, I had bought 50 acres and tried to expand strawberry farming, hoping to benefit from a major international exposition, but that venture lost money. I had to break up the partnership and start small again, gradually recovering my losses. The Depression was tough but I was able to keep farming.

explicit_text: In 1915, I expanded my farm to 50 acres and hoped to make money because of the World's Fair that year. Unfortunately, people did not buy as much as expected, and our five-partner enterprise lost money. We broke up the partnership, and I started a smaller farm again. The World's Fair was a big event but did not bring the financial boost I had hoped for.
implicit_text: In 1915, I expanded my farm to 50 acres and hoped to make money because of a major international exposition held that year. Unfortunately, people did not buy as much as expected, and our five-partner enterprise lost money. We broke up the partnership, and I started a smaller farm again. That event was a big deal but did not bring the financial boost I had hoped for.

explicit_text: World War II was a very difficult time for us. After Pearl Harbor was attacked, I was shocked and worried about the safety of Japanese people in America. We had to evacuate our farm and go to the Salinas Assembly Center and then to the Poston War Relocation Center in Arizona. We lost everything we had worked for, and some people were hostile toward us. It was a painful disruption in our lives.
implicit_text: The global conflict of the 1940s was a very difficult time for us. After the surprise attack on a naval base in the Pacific, I was shocked and worried about the safety of Japanese people in America. We had to evacuate our farm and go to an assembly center in California and then to a relocation center in Arizona. We lost everything we had worked for, and some people were hostile toward us. It was a painful disruption in our lives.

explicit_text: When the Japanese attacked Pearl Harbor in December 1941, I was shocked and feared for the Japanese community in Hawaii and the mainland. Although I didn’t think we were in immediate danger here, tensions rose quickly, and some people even pointed guns at us after the war started. The attack changed everything and led to our forced evacuation.
implicit_text: When the surprise attack on the naval base in Hawaii happened in December 1941, I was shocked and feared for the Japanese community in the Pacific islands and the mainland. Although I didn’t think we were in immediate danger here, tensions rose quickly, and some people even pointed guns at us after the war started. The attack changed everything and led to our forced evacuation.

explicit_text: After the war broke out, we were sent to the Salinas Assembly Center as part of the forced evacuation of Japanese Americans. It was crowded and not very nice, but we tried not to grieve because so many were in the same situation. The food was adequate, but the toilets were terrible and left a lasting bad impression. We lined up for meals and lived with many inconveniences there.
implicit_text: After the war broke out, we were sent to a temporary assembly center in California as part of the forced evacuation of Japanese Americans. It was crowded and not very nice, but we tried not to grieve because so many were in the same situation. The food was adequate, but the toilets were terrible and left a lasting bad impression. We lined up for meals and lived with many inconveniences there.

explicit_text: After Salinas, we were moved to the Poston War Relocation Center in Arizona. Life there was better; we had plenty of water and even a swimming pool, though the weather was very hot, around 102 degrees. I helped cook and built an entertainment stand from scrap lumber. Despite the hardships, we tried to keep busy and maintain some normalcy.
implicit_text: After the assembly center, we were moved to a relocation center in Arizona. Life there was better; we had plenty of water and even a swimming pool, though the weather was very hot, around 102 degrees. I helped cook and built an entertainment stand from scrap lumber. Despite the hardships, we tried to keep busy and maintain some normalcy.

explicit_text: The Nisei, or second-generation Japanese Americans, were required to sign a loyalty questionnaire and many were sent to camps like Poston. I did not have to sign because I was Issei. I felt it was unfortunate that the Nisei were put in camps, but I understood it was unavoidable at the time. Many Nisei joined the U.S. Army from the camps and served overseas. My eldest son was not taken to camp because of skin trouble but later died in Chicago.
implicit_text: The second-generation Japanese Americans were required to sign a loyalty questionnaire and many were sent to camps like the one in Arizona. I did not have to sign because I was first-generation. I felt it was unfortunate that the younger generation were put in camps, but I understood it was unavoidable at the time. Many of them joined the U.S. Army from the camps and served overseas. My eldest son was not taken to camp because of skin trouble but later died in Chicago.

explicit_text: My eldest son died rather young in Chicago. He had skin trouble which exempted him from being taken to the camps during the war. After the war, I appealed to have my other son discharged from the army to help with farming. Chicago was where my son spent his last days, away from our California home.
implicit_text: My eldest son died rather young in a large Midwestern city. He had skin trouble which exempted him from being taken to the camps during the war. After the war, I appealed to have my other son discharged from the army to help with farming. That city was where my son spent his last days, away from our California home.
